challenges.situation: Monday's raise thrilled you. By Friday you've learned what a colleague got, and the same number now feels like an insult.

options.text: Push to renegotiate immediately so you're not behind your colleague.
options.feedback: You're chasing rank, not value. Even if you win, the wiring just finds the next person ahead — it's a race with no finish line.

options.text: Name what happened: 'My situation didn't change — I just found a comparison,' then ask whether you actually wanted their life.
options.feedback: Catching the swap in real time breaks its grip. The facts of your life are unchanged; only the measuring stick moved. Usually you don't want their life — you just don't want to feel behind.

options.text: Decide the raise was meaningless and feel quietly bitter about the whole thing.
options.feedback: That's letting comparison set your worth — the Duryodhana path. You lost nothing real, yet you've surrendered the joy of something you genuinely won.

challenges.situation: A prince who owns wealth, an army, and a waiting crown cannot eat or sleep — purely because his cousins' new palace is finer than his.
challenges.question: Which force is driving this?

options.feedback: He hasn't lost a single thing. His life is exactly as rich as the day before. Nothing real was taken.
His worth was tied to being ahead. The cousins' success costs him nothing real, yet it costs him everything — because his satisfaction depends on ranking, not on what he has.
Ambition builds; this only burns. The tell is that someone else's gain diminishes his joy even though he lost nothing — that's defending a rank, not pursuing a life.

challenges.context: Two people feel the sting of watching a peer pull ahead.

scenarioA.label: Comparison as verdict
scenarioA.text: 'They're ahead, so I'm worthless.' The sting sets their self-esteem and sours everything they have.

scenarioB.label: Comparison as information
scenarioB.text: 'How did they do that?' The sting becomes a question that reveals a path and lights a fire.

challenges.explanation: The same act produces opposite results. Noticing others isn't the danger — letting the noticing set your worth is. Used as information, comparison is fuel; used as a verdict, it's poison.

challenges.front: If I just achieve a bit more, I'll finally feel ahead and the comparison ache will stop.
challenges.back: There is always someone ahead, and hedonic adaptation means every gain becomes the new baseline within days. Comparison isn't measurement — it's a status hunt that never ends. The only comparison you can win is to past-you, where you control both data points and the finish line is yours.

principle.text: Comparison is not measurement — it's a status hunt, and the hunt never ends.
principle.subtext: There is always someone ahead. So when your satisfaction depends on ranking, you've signed up for a race with no finish line, where every win just reveals the next person to lose to.

reflection: Name three things you have right now that a younger version of you would have been thrilled to reach. When did you last actually feel their value — and what comparison stole it?
